text.title: django-api Core Rules
text.summary: Eight testable rules for Django REST APIs (DRF or Ninja).

rule.statement: Pick DRF OR Ninja at project bootstrap and lock it. Mixing DRF and Ninja in the same Django app is forbidden.
rule.rationale: URL routing, authentication context, and OpenAPI generation diverge between the two stacks. Mixing them multiplies failure surface and produces inconsistent client SDKs.

rule.statement: Views contain no business logic. The view: parses request via the input serializer/schema → calls a service function → returns the output serializer/schema. Business logic (DB writes beyond ORM saves, side effects, multi-step transactions) lives in `<app>/services.py`.
rule.rationale: Fat views cannot be unit-tested without an HTTP request and cannot be reused from Celery tasks or management commands. Service layer is the only path to consistent business rules.

rule.statement: Each resource has separate Create/Update input serializers and a Detail/List output serializer. Never use one serializer for both directions.
rule.rationale: A single bidirectional serializer mixes write-only with read-only fields, makes permission-based field gating impossible, and causes refactor cascades when one side needs to change.

rule.statement: `Meta.fields = "__all__"` is FORBIDDEN on every user-facing serializer or schema. List fields explicitly; mark id/uid/created_at/updated_at as read_only_fields.
rule.rationale: Adding a column to the model silently exposes it through the API. Past leaks include password hashes, JWT secrets, and internal foreign-key IDs. Explicit allowlist is the only safe path.

rule.statement: simplejwt configured with access token TTL ≤ 15 minutes, refresh token TTL ≤ 7 days, ROTATE_REFRESH_TOKENS=true, BLACKLIST_AFTER_ROTATION=true. Tokens transmitted via HttpOnly cookies (preferred) or Authorization header.
rule.rationale: Short access tokens cap the blast radius of a leaked token; rotation + blacklist prevent refresh-token replay. localStorage is XSS-readable; HttpOnly cookies are not.

rule.statement: DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES = ['rest_framework.permissions.IsAuthenticated']. AllowAny is forbidden as a default; endpoints that must be public opt in explicitly.
rule.rationale: A locked-down default means a developer who forgets to set permissions cannot accidentally ship a public endpoint. The opposite default has caused multiple public-data-leak incidents.

rule.statement: Resource-ownership checks live in `has_object_permission(request, view, obj)`. `has_permission(request, view)` handles only method + auth gating. Skipping object-level checks is forbidden when the resource is user/org-owned.
rule.rationale: has_permission runs before object fetch (cheap, can't see the object). has_object_permission runs after get_object() and is the only correct place for ownership checks. Putting ownership in has_permission either over-fetches or misses the check entirely.

rule.statement: Throttle by scope: at minimum `anon`, `user`, `burst` (for write endpoints), `login` (for auth endpoints). List endpoints use cursor pagination ordered by `(-created_at, -id)` when the dataset can grow unbounded; page-number pagination otherwise.
rule.rationale: A single anon/user limit can't distinguish a read flood from a login brute-force. Cursor pagination on an unindexed field causes table scans; the -id tie-breaker ensures stable ordering across same-timestamp rows.
